text: So this is a robotic-assisted Ivor Lewis esophagectomy we're doing on a sixty-seven-year-old man who had an EGD for dysphagia and was found to have an adenocarcinoma at the gastroesophageal junction. He underwent induction chemotherapy, followed by chemoradiation, and then was taken to the operating room.
The surgeon begins the procedure in the abdomen, inserting a camera port approximately two centimeters above the umbilicus.

The Nathanson retractor is used to retract the liver. A 12mm port is placed in the right upper quadrant, and two 8mm ports are inserted in the left upper quadrant. Occasionally, a 5mm assist port is added for suctioning.
The surgeon begins harvesting an omental flap by detaching the greater omentum from the transverse colon, using careful dissection techniques to separate these structures.
And this can be somewhat tricky. I find it a little easier to do this laparoscopically, to be honest, than using the robot, but with perseverance you get there.
The surgeon locates a pedicle, vein, and artery branching from the gastroepiploic artery to supply the flap. A vessel sealing device is used to divide these structures efficiently.

The surgeon uses a tip-up grasper through the furthest right-hand port for retraction, while a bipolar dissector is operated through the other right-hand port. A fenestrated bipolar is held in the left hand. The piece of omentum intended for the omental flap is visible, along with the vascular pedicle.
The surgeon continues to detach the greater omentum from the transverse colon, ensuring not to harvest excessive omentum to avoid bulkiness, especially in patients with more adipose tissue.

The omental flap is visible, flipped over the stomach in a cephalad direction. The surgeon continues to divide the omentum towards the pylorus, taking care around the pedicle of the right gastroepiploic artery.
The surgeon uses bipolar cautery to dissect through the fatty tissue, revealing the vein associated with the gastroepiploic artery pedicle.
The surgeon divides the short gastric vessels along the greater curvature of the stomach using a vessel sealing device, ensuring careful separation of these vessels from surrounding tissue.
And the vessel sealer seems very reliable for this. I've never had a problem with bleeding from an incompletely sealed vessel.

An assistant uses a 5mm grasper to retract the pancreas and colon, while the surgeon takes down additional attachments between the omentum and spleen. The stomach is retracted downward towards the patient's umbilicus, providing a clear view of the surgical field.
The surgeon approaches the left crus of the diaphragm, ensuring that neither the left nor right crus is divided during the procedure.
The surgeon addresses the remaining short gastric attachments, avoiding extensive dissection near the hiatus to prevent pneumothorax and maintain pneumoperitoneum.

The surgeon lifts the stomach anteriorly, exposing the left gastric vein and artery. The left gastric vein is dissected and divided using a vessel sealing device or hemlock clips, while the artery is not divided with the vessel sealing device.

The surgeon begins dissecting the left gastric artery, approaching from the right side of the stomach. The lesser omentum is opened to access the lesser sac. A large replaced left hepatic artery is visible, and the surgeon demonstrates management of this anatomical variation.

The robotic instruments are used to dissect and preserve the visible branch of the left gastric artery to the stomach. The surgeon skeletonizes the artery, identifying and dividing the branch while ensuring careful handling due to its larger size. Multiple branches may be present, requiring cautious dissection.
The surgeon applies a hemlock clip to the proximal side of the vessel before using a vessel sealing device to cut the artery.
The surgeon identifies tissue that appears to be vagal branches near the common artery. The tissue is then mobilized cephalad and included with the specimen for removal.
The surgeon identifies the right crus of the diaphragm and proceeds to dissect tissue down to the level of the descending aorta. Celiac axis lymph nodes are being excised during this process.

The surgeon divides the fat tissue to expose the common hepatic artery, using careful blunt dissection to create and separate tissue planes. The left gastric artery is visible, extending towards the portal vein. The dissection continues down to the inferior vena cava (IVC), with caution to avoid the cephalad-positioned portal vein.

The surgeon uses a tracheal suction catheter connected to a five millimeter sucker through a five millimeter port to remove the tissue as a separate specimen, allowing controlled suction during the procedure.
The surgeon positions the suction tip and divides the tissue at the level of the crow's foot. Following this, robotic staplers are used to create a conduit.

The surgeon uses a green load stapler to create a narrow gastric conduit, approximately five centimeters wide. The tip-up grasper provides an estimation of the conduit size. Downward retraction is applied to elongate the stomach, ensuring the nasogastric tube has been withdrawn by the anesthesiologist.

The surgeon carefully fires the staple line to divide the conduit from the proximal stomach, ensuring no twisting occurs. After division, the tip of the conduit is sutured to the stomach remnant to facilitate its elevation into the chest.

The surgeon assesses the perfusion of the gastric conduit using indocyanine green dye, which is mixed with saline and injected. The dye visibly reaches the stomach within approximately twenty seconds, providing a real-time visual confirmation of perfusion.

The surgeon assesses the perfusion of the esophageal conduit, noting that the entire conduit is well-perfused except for the top portion, which will be removed during the chest anastomosis. A stitch is placed at the watershed area to guide the placement of the anastomosis below it. The omental flap is also visibly well-perfused.

The surgeon performs a hiatal dissection using robotic instruments. The patient already has a feeding jejunostomy tube in place due to pre-existing dysphagia, eliminating the need for additional laparoscopic placement of a J-tube.

The surgeon carefully dissects tissue down to the aorta, aiming to achieve a wide radial margin while avoiding the esophageal wall. The dissection approaches the pericardium, with potential entry into the left pleura during this process.
The surgeon dissects along the pericardium, extending the dissection towards the carina. The inferior pulmonary veins are visible and require careful attention during this process.

The surgeon mobilizes approximately six to seven centimeters of tissue up into the hiatus, facilitating easier dissection from the chest. A chest tube is inserted through one of the abdominal ports, using a Blake drain, after entering the left pleura.

The surgeon ensures the entire drain is positioned within the chest cavity to prevent drainage of peritoneal fluid. The tip of the esophageal conduit and the harvested omental flap are sewn to the remnant of the stomach, preparing them to be brought up into the chest for the chest component of the procedure.

And lastly, botulinum toxin injection, which may or may not help. I don't typically do a formal pylori myotomy on these patients. I don't think you need it with a narrow conduit. Whether or not Botox works, I think the evidence is a bit plus or minus. But it's easy to do, and so we often just inject it. The other way you can do this possibly better is endoscopically with an endoscope at the end of the case.

The surgical team rotates the patient into a left lateral acute position to begin the chest portion of the esophagectomy. Ports are placed for thoracic access: a camera port is positioned anteriorly over the sixth rib, an eight-millimeter port is placed posteriorly, another port is positioned inferiorly, and a final port is placed above the fourth or third rib.

The surgeon takes down the inferior pulmonary ligament, allowing the lung to fall forward while staying close to the pericardium. The area of previous dissection from the abdominal portion is visible, and the tissue at the hiatus is prepared for en bloc resection with the esophageal specimen.
The surgeon dissects the pleura on both the anterior and posterior aspects of the esophagus, progressing upwards until reaching the bronchus, where a bipolar dissecting device is utilized.
The surgeon uses a precise technique to divide the esophagus just below the azygos vein, focusing on the gastroesophageal junction due to the presence of a C-word type two tumor.

The surgeon divides the pleura along the posterior aspect of the esophagus, reaching the level of the azagus vein. The thoracic duct is identified and dissected along with the esophageal specimen, with the aorta visible deep to the duct.
The surgeon approaches the thoracic duct, preparing to divide it using hemlock clips for ligation.

The surgeon carefully dissects and removes the subcarinal lymph nodes en bloc with the esophageal specimen, staying close to the pericardium to minimize bleeding. The nodes are visibly vascularized, and care is taken to avoid fracturing them during removal.
The surgeon identifies the thoracic duct and clips the cephalad aspect of it using surgical clips.

The surgeon lifts and laterally retracts the esophagus, using a vessel sealing device to detach remaining attachments to the aorta and pericardium. The device is maneuvered through the posterior port for optimal angle, ensuring careful avoidance of the left main stem bronchus.

The left main stem bronchus is visible with the posterior membranous portion and the left-sided endotracheal tube. The subcarinal nodes are elevated off the esophagus, exposing the esophageal musculature for division.

The surgeon uses endocyanin green and near-infrared imaging to confirm the esophagus is well-perfused before division. The esophagus lights up entirely, indicating suitable perfusion for division. Endoshears are set to a cutting mode for this procedure.
After dividing the esophagus, the surgeon enlarges the anterior incision to remove the specimen. The conduit is pulled up and released from the proximal stomach.

The surgeon sutures the tip of the omentum to the proximal stomach to facilitate pulling the conduit up. Manual assistance from an assist port is used to avoid excessive tension on the conduit, preventing potential tearing.

The surgeon carefully pulls the conduit up to the level of the azygos vein without grasping it directly. A purse string anastomosis is performed using two 3-0 Prolene sutures. The first suture is a running horizontal mattress, ensuring inclusion of both muscle and mucosa, and is run circumferentially.

The surgeon prepares to use a 25mm circular stapler for esophageal anastomosis, avoiding placing the anvil in the esophagus lumen initially. The assistant passes the anvil, which is then positioned using a needle driver.

Be very, very careful tying proline, particularly Threo, because it will break. And you have no tactile sensation about how much tension you're putting on the monofilament. And so you really have to almost look to see the stretch in the suture. And stop pulling just before it's about to snap. But it can snap. Just be careful of it. So this is the second purse string.

The surgeon ties down the suture and creates a small gastrotomy at the tip of the conduit. The stapler is inserted through the conduit and exits at the back wall along the greater curvature. A suction device is kept nearby to manage any spillage of gastric contents upon opening the conduit.
The camera is repositioned to the cephalad-most port, providing a clear view of the stapler as it exits the back of the gastric conduit.

The surgeon inserts the ILS stapler into the conduit, advancing the spear through the back wall of the stomach. The camera positioned high provides a clear view of the spear's exit point. The surgeon uses a ProGrasp in the right hand to manipulate the anvil, as it offers better grip strength compared to other instruments.

The assistant fires the stapler to complete the anastomosis, ensuring no fat or extraneous tissue is incorporated. The surgeon checks the completeness of the doughnuts formed by the staple line, carefully dividing any excess tissue while avoiding proximity to the circular staple line to prevent ischemia in the stomach.

The surgeon over-sews the staple line, focusing on the more ischemic end to imbricate it. Moving to the hiatus, two or three tacking sutures are placed using silk or Vicryl to secure the stomach to the diaphragm edge, aiming to prevent herniation. The surgeon completes the over-sewing and positions the omental flap to wrap around the conduit, providing protection from the airway in case of a leak.
The surgeon secures the omental flap to the pleura or mediastinal tissue to prevent migration. A chest tube is inserted, and the cavity is irrigated before inflating the lung.
